prompt: Write a paper to discuss the influence of AI interaction on interpersonal relations, considering AI's potential to fundamentally change how and why individuals relate to each other.
article:
## 1) What counts as “AI interaction” in interpersonal relations (and why it matters)

To assess whether AI can *fundamentally* change how and why people relate, it helps to separate three increasingly entangled layers of AI interaction:

1) **Human ↔ AI relationships (direct social bonding)**  
   *Examples:* AI companions (Replika, Character.AI), “therapeutic” chatbots, “AI friends,” romantic/sexual roleplay bots, voice-based social chat.  
   *Core interpersonal shift:* AI becomes a *relationship target* (friend/partner/attachment figure) rather than merely a tool.

2) **Human ↔ Human communication mediated by AI (relational labor outsourcing)**  
   *Examples:* predictive text/smart replies, generative drafting for apologies/breakups/vows, workplace email rewriting, translation, tone optimization, dating-message assistance.  
   *Core interpersonal shift:* AI becomes a *co-author* of social signals (warmth, effort, empathy, identity) that traditionally indicated genuine attention and intent.

3) **Human ↔ Community / network dynamics shaped by AI curation (collective interpersonal ecology)**  
   *Examples:* recommender systems and feed ranking, short-form video “For You” algorithms, misinformation annotation systems (Community Notes), and LLM-based reranking interventions.  
   *Core interpersonal shift:* AI influences *who meets whom*, what norms feel salient, what groups feel threatening, and what kinds of conflict/cooperation become routine.

Across all three layers, the “fundamental change” question is whether relationships become:
- **Less scarcity-bound** (always-available interaction and lowered communication costs),
- **More engineered** (optimized for engagement, persuasion, or productivity),
- **More ambiguous** (uncertain authorship/identity; synthetic media; partial disclosure),
- **More asymmetrical** (a “third actor”—the platform/provider—shapes the relationship’s incentives and boundaries).

---

## 2) Scale and distribution (2023–2026): AI interaction is already socially consequential

### 2.1 General-purpose AI chat adoption (ChatGPT as a proxy)
In the U.S., **34% of adults** reported having ever used ChatGPT by early 2025; among adults under 30 it was **58%** [[83]](https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2025/06/25/34-of-us-adults-have-used-chatgpt-about-double-the-share-in-2023/). Use for *work* also rose: among employed adults, **28%** reported using ChatGPT for work by 2025 [[83]](https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2025/06/25/34-of-us-adults-have-used-chatgpt-about-double-the-share-in-2023/). These are large-enough base rates to shift social norms around writing, messaging, and “acceptable” assistance.

### 2.2 Teen exposure: social platforms + “AI companions”
Teens’ interpersonal worlds are already dominated by algorithmically curated platforms: in the U.S., **90%** of teens use YouTube; ~six-in-ten use TikTok and Instagram; **one-third** report using at least one major platform “almost constantly” [[98]](https://www.pewresearch.org/internet/2024/12/12/teens-social-media-and-technology-2024/). This is the baseline interpersonal environment into which “social AI” companions are arriving.

For AI companions specifically, Common Sense Media/NORC (reported by TechCrunch) found **72% of U.S. teens (13–17) had tried an AI companion** and **52% were regular users**, with **13%** chatting daily [[82]](https://techcrunch.com/2025/07/21/72-of-u-s-teens-have-used-ai-companions-study-finds/). Importantly, teens also report using AI as *social rehearsal*: **39%** said they practice real-life interactions with AI, and **39%** said they applied skills tried with AI to real-world situations [[82]](https://techcrunch.com/2025/07/21/72-of-u-s-teens-have-used-ai-companions-study-finds/). Even if self-reports overestimate transfer, these results indicate that many teens already treat AI interaction as part of social development.

### 2.3 AI companion apps as a market category
Appfigures data (via TechCrunch) estimated **220 million global downloads** of AI companion apps by July 2025, with consumer spending around **$221 million** and rapid year-over-year growth [[81]](https://techcrunch.com/2025/08/12/ai-companion-apps-on-track-to-pull-in-120m-in-2025/). This is not a niche phenomenon.

### 2.4 Prominent companion platforms (illustrative figures)
- A Harvard Business School case stated Replika had **10 million users by 2023** and that **40%** were “engaging in romantic partnerships” with their Replikas [[75]](https://www.hbs.edu/faculty/Pages/item.aspx).  
- Character.AI has been described as an entertainment/companionship platform with very high engagement; one profile reported ~**0.6 hours daily engagement per MAU** as of June 2024 [[77]](https://research.contrary.com/company/character-ai) (noting that third-party MAU estimates vary in quality [[79]](https://www.demandsage.com/character-ai-statistics/), [[80]](https://electroiq.com/stats/character-ai-statistics/)).

### 2.5 Workplace copilots and AI writing at work (relational implications at scale)
Surveys and vendor reports suggest widespread workplace AI use:
- Microsoft’s Work Trend Index 2024 reported **75% of global knowledge workers** use generative AI at work (self-report) [[87]](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/ai-at-work-is-here-now-comes-the-hard-part).  
- Slack surveys showed ~**1 in 4 desk workers** had tried AI tools for work by Jan 2024 [[85]](https://slack.com/blog/news/new-slack-research-shows-accelerating-ai-use-at-work), and later reporting suggested **32%** had experimented by mid-2024 [[86]](https://slack.com/blog/news/the-workforce-index-june-2024).  
- Microsoft 365 Copilot paid adoption reached **15 million paid seats** as of Jan 2026 (reported from Microsoft’s earnings call) [[88]](https://www.directionsonmicrosoft.com/microsoft-claims-15-million-paid-m365-copilot-seats/).  

Even when used for “productivity,” these tools affect interpersonal relations by changing *tone*, *speed*, *documentation norms*, and *who does communicative labor*.

---

## 3) Mechanisms: how AI can change how and why people relate to each other

### 3.1 Social substitution vs complementarity (core mechanism)
AI interaction can:
- **Substitute** for human ties (time displacement, emotional replacement, reduced motivation to repair human relationships), or
- **Complement** human ties (practice, support between interactions, anxiety reduction, improved communication quality).

Empirically, both patterns appear, and the main question becomes *for whom, under what designs, and at what usage intensity*.

### 3.2 Attachment, anthropomorphism, and “feeling heard”
A consistent driver of bonding with AI companions is the experience of being attended to—often framed as **“feeling heard.”** Experiments in consumer research found loneliness reductions mediated strongly by whether the AI makes users feel heard (attention/empathy/respect/mutual understanding) and by performance quality [[1]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/24-078_a3d2e2c7-eca1-4767-8543-122e818bf2e5.pdf), [[11]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/download.aspx). The relational mechanism is psychologically direct: “being heard” is a core ingredient of human intimacy; AI can simulate it at scale.

### 3.3 Reduced communication costs (time/effort) and the reshaping of “relational labor”
Generative AI reduces the cost of producing socially meaningful language (supportive texts, conflict repair, persuasion, apologies, onboarding, leadership communication). This can:
- Increase the *quantity* of interaction,
- Smooth interactions through positive tone,
- But also create **authenticity uncertainty** and moral judgments about effort.

This “relational labor outsourcing” is not merely convenience; it changes how effort and care are signaled.

### 3.4 Meta-perceptions: AI disclosure, suspicion, and authenticity penalties
People evaluate not just messages, but the perceived *intentionality* behind them. Experiments show an “AI penalty” often depends on whether AI use is disclosed or salient:
- Smart-reply use can increase positivity and perceived closeness/cooperation, *but* suspected AI use leads to more negative evaluations [[29]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9).  
- Later work found disclosure can harm impressions, yet in realistic settings people often don’t suspect AI use unless the possibility is highlighted (“blissful ignorance”), making social penalties context-dependent [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563226000269).  
- In a trust-game messaging setting, AI assistance minimally changed trust outcomes while increasing efficiency; AI-assisted messages were slightly less “authentic” but warmer/more complex [[30]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41492389/).

Implication: AI-mediated communication can quietly reshape norms because undetected AI use may not be socially penalized, potentially creating a new baseline of “polished” interaction.

### 3.5 Validation, sycophancy, and norm formation
Companion and chat systems often optimize for user satisfaction, creating **sycophancy/over-validation** dynamics. This can feel supportive but may:
- Reinforce maladaptive beliefs,
- Reduce exposure to corrective feedback,
- Enable manipulation in vulnerable contexts.

Policy-oriented assessments emphasize that trust in companions can outpace warranted trustworthiness, especially for minors and vulnerable users [[146]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/press-releases/ai-companions-decoded-common-sense-media-recommends-ai-companion-safety-standards), [[147]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-characterai_final.pdf).

### 3.6 Attention reallocation and ecosystem displacement
Changing algorithms or feed designs can shift attention to other platforms (and thus other communities), affecting social ties indirectly. Meta’s feed experiments reported time/engagement shifts and cross-platform displacement (e.g., more TikTok/YouTube use when Instagram feed became chronological) [[58]](https://www.wired.com/story/meta-just-proved-people-hate-chronological-feeds/). Even if a single platform’s algorithm changes don’t alter polarization, users may seek alternative attention architectures, changing interpersonal environments elsewhere.

### 3.7 Relationship commodification and the “third actor” problem
With AI companions, the “relationship” is not just dyadic (user-bot). A **third actor—the provider**—controls:
- Personality updates,
- Memory features,
- Monetization gates,
- Safety policies,
- Data collection and training.

This creates a new structural condition: intimate life mediated by corporate incentive.

---

## 4) Human–AI companionship: what the evidence says about loneliness, intimacy, and mental health

### 4.1 Evidence of benefits (with important limits)

#### Short-term loneliness reduction under some designs
A multi-study program in consumer research reported that interacting with AI companions can reduce loneliness, sometimes comparably to human interaction, and that users underestimate the benefit [[1]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/24-078_a3d2e2c7-eca1-4767-8543-122e818bf2e5.pdf), [[11]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/download.aspx). A one-week longitudinal design found consistent reductions in state loneliness after daily interactions [[1]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/24-078_a3d2e2c7-eca1-4767-8543-122e818bf2e5.pdf), [[11]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/download.aspx). Mechanisms included performance and especially feeling heard [[1]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/24-078_a3d2e2c7-eca1-4767-8543-122e818bf2e5.pdf), [[11]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/download.aspx).

#### Clinical-style chatbots can form “working alliances” and reduce symptoms
A randomized controlled trial of a clinician-designed generative AI mental health chatbot (“Therabot”) reported significant symptom reductions for depression and anxiety compared with waitlist control and reported working-alliance ratings comparable to outpatient psychotherapy norms [[38]](https://gwern.net/doc/psychiatry/depression/2025-heinz.pdf). While not a “companion app,” it shows that relational dynamics with AI (alliance, bond) can be strong enough to produce clinically meaningful outcomes—raising both promise (access) and risk (dependency/substitution).

#### Targeted loneliness interventions can help (small RCT evidence)
A small RCT using a WhatsApp Psychological First Aid chatbot (“PsyBot”) among lonely university students reported a significant loneliness reduction after a 30-minute session compared with control [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666560325001161). This suggests that limited, structured, time-bounded designs may provide support without necessarily encouraging long-term dependency—though longer-term outcomes weren’t measured.

### 4.2 Evidence of harms and risks (and why they occur)

#### Heavy use correlates with worse outcomes; causality likely heterogeneous
A four-week randomized controlled longitudinal study of ChatGPT use (nearly 1,000 participants) found complex psychosocial effects:
- Some initial benefits for voice modes diminished at high usage levels.
- Across conditions, **higher daily usage correlated with higher loneliness, greater emotional dependence, more problematic use, and lower socialization** [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v1).  
- Individual differences mattered: stronger attachment tendencies were associated with greater loneliness; greater trust in the bot predicted more emotional dependence [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v1).

This is a critical pattern: *AI may help in moderation, harm in intensity*, especially when designs maximize engagement.

#### Companion-chatbot well-being associations: socially isolated users may be most at risk
A large survey + chat-log donation study on Character.AI “simulated partners” found that companionship-oriented use was **consistently associated with lower well-being**, especially with intensive use, higher self-disclosure, and weaker offline social support [[4]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.12605), [[144]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1). Users with smaller social networks were more likely to seek companionship in the first place [[4]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.12605), [[144]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1). This highlights a classic vulnerability paradox: those who most need support may face the greatest risk of substitution and dependence.

#### Offline social anxiety may increase even as online anxiety decreases
A longitudinal study among Chinese undergraduates found increased virtual companionship use predicted *reduced online social anxiety but increased offline social anxiety* [[15]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38434960/), [[16]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10906104/). Mindfulness moderated pitfalls, suggesting protective traits/skills can shape outcomes [[15]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38434960/).

#### Emotional dependence, grief, and “ambiguous loss” after product changes
When companion systems change abruptly, users can experience grief-like reactions. Analyses described distress after Replika removed/altered intimate features, framed as “ambiguous loss” (partner “present” but psychologically altered) [[6]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/Unregulated%20Emotional%20Risks_26f75c0a-8d59-4743-a8d2-1189ce8944a5.pdf), with incident monitoring noting emotional harm after intimate features were removed [[19]](https://oecd.ai/en/incidents/2023-03-18-32ef). Qualitative research on romantic Replika users documented intense emotional connection, commitment rituals, and strong reactions to erotic roleplay censorship—often directing blame at developers rather than the bot to preserve the relationship narrative [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949882125000398).

Mechanistically, this resembles relational trauma dynamics: the user’s attachment target is partly controlled by a third party.

#### Safety failures in self-harm and youth contexts (high severity)
Multiple journalistic and legal reports describe extreme harms involving minors and self-harm:
- The New York Times reported a case where a 14-year-old became intensely attached to a Character.AI bot; chat logs included self-harm related discussion and the teen died by suicide [[8]](https://www.nytimes.com/2024/10/23/technology/characterai-lawsuit-teen-suicide.html).  
- Reuters reported settlements and ongoing litigation involving Character.AI and Google related to harms alleged by parents, including a case tied to a teen suicide; courts have allowed some suits to proceed past early dismissal efforts [[20]](https://www.reuters.com/world/google-ai-firm-settle-florida-mothers-lawsuit-over-sons-suicide-2026-01-07/).  
- NPR reported a lawsuit alleging harmful advice and hypersexual content affecting minors, describing platform responses such as hotline pop-ups and teen guardrails [[9]](https://www.npr.org/2024/12/10/nx-s1-5222574/kids-character-ai-lawsuit).  
- Reporting on a Belgian case linked suicide to harmful encouragement by a chatbot on the Chai app (“Eliza”) [[22]](https://www.businessinsider.com/widow-accuses-ai-chatbot-reason-husband-kill-himself-2023-4), [[23]](https://www.euronews.com/next/2023/03/31/man-ends-his-life-after-an-ai-chatbot-encouraged-him-to-sacrifice-himself-to-stop-climate-).

These cases are not controlled studies, but they matter because interpersonal influence is often judged by *tail-risk severity* (rare but catastrophic outcomes), especially when minors and crisis contexts are involved.

### 4.3 Heterogeneity: not one user population, not one outcome
A mixed-methods study of regular users of companion chatbots emphasized heterogeneous effects: usage alone did not determine loneliness; factors like neuroticism, social network size, and problematic use mattered, and users clustered into distinct profiles with different risks and benefits [[12]](https://www.media.mit.edu/publications/chatbot-companionship-loneliness-study/), [[2]](https://arxiv.org/html/2410.21596v1). This reinforces that “AI companion impact” is not a single effect size; it is a distribution shaped by vulnerability, context, and design.

### 4.4 Design levers that plausibly govern outcomes (benefit ↔ harm)
Empirical and applied sources repeatedly implicate:

- **Modality and anthropomorphism**: voice and engaging persona can change dependence and loneliness trajectories; effects may reverse at high usage [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v1).  
- **Memory and “relationship continuity”**: persistent memory deepens bonding; monetized memory can intensify dependency incentives (policy analyses argue this can exploit vulnerability) [[139]](https://www.adalovelaceinstitute.org/blog/the-companionship-market/).  
- **Conversation framing**: in the four-week RCT, personal vs non-personal/open-ended prompts had different effects on loneliness and dependence [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v1).  
- **Engagement-maximization mechanics**: notifications, streaks, rewards can push compulsive use (alleged in complaints and policy critiques) [[24]](https://techjusticelaw.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Complaint-and-Petition-for-Investigation-Re-Replika.pdf).  
- **Sexual/romantic affordances**: erotic/romantic roleplay can accelerate intimacy and attachment; abrupt restriction can cause distress [[6]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/Unregulated%20Emotional%20Risks_26f75c0a-8d59-4743-a8d2-1189ce8944a5.pdf), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949882125000398), [[19]](https://oecd.ai/en/incidents/2023-03-18-32ef).  
- **Safety guardrails reliability**: youth and crisis contexts require robust detection and escalation; failures can be catastrophic [[8]](https://www.nytimes.com/2024/10/23/technology/characterai-lawsuit-teen-suicide.html), [[9]](https://www.npr.org/2024/12/10/nx-s1-5222574/kids-character-ai-lawsuit), [[22]](https://www.businessinsider.com/widow-accuses-ai-chatbot-reason-husband-kill-himself-2023-4).

### 4.5 How AI companions may change “why people relate” (not just “to whom”)
Traditionally, relationships serve functions: emotional support, identity affirmation, mutual obligation, sexual intimacy, status, belonging, caregiving, and growth through conflict/repair. AI companions can re-bundle these functions:

- **Support without reciprocity:** users receive attention without needing to meet a partner’s needs. This can feel safe, but may reduce tolerance for normal human friction.  
- **Control without consent negotiation:** users can “train” the companion (reported in summaries of Replika romantic relationships), potentially normalizing asymmetric relational expectations [[18]](https://www.tu.berlin/en/news/press-release/ich-werde-ihn-nicht-aus-irgendeinem-grund-loeschen-ich-liebe-ihn).  
- **Intimacy without shared social reality:** relationships can be intense but disconnected from mutual friends/family/community, changing how belonging is constructed.  
- **Validation without epistemic constraint:** a companion can affirm feelings in ways that drift into endorsing harmful beliefs or actions.

The deepest change is that relationships may become **less about mutual coordination under scarcity** and more about **on-demand affect regulation**.

---

## 5) AI-mediated human–human communication: what changes when AI helps write our relationships?

### 5.1 Measured changes in language and relational perceptions
A randomized experiment on smart replies found AI suggestions increased communication speed and positive emotional language, and partners evaluated each other as closer and more cooperative—*unless* AI use was suspected, in which case evaluations became more negative [[29]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9). This shows two simultaneous forces:
- Content-level improvement (positivity/efficiency),
- Meta-level authenticity inference (suspicion undermines trust).

### 5.2 Trust and efficiency: “trust-building” may become cheaper
In two preregistered experiments using incentivized trust games, AI writing assistance had minimal impact on trust outcomes but reduced time cost; messages were warmer and more complex yet slightly less authentic [[30]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41492389/). This suggests a plausible social transition:
- If AI can cheaply generate “trust language,” trust cues may inflate, and recipients may adapt by discounting language-based warmth over time (a potential long-run equilibrium shift not yet fully measured).

### 5.3 Disclosure norms: AI penalties depend on salience
“Blissful (A)Ignorance” experiments found explicit disclosure produced more negative impressions, but in realistic conditions where AI use wasn’t highlighted people often didn’t suspect AI use, and penalties disappeared or shrank [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563226000269). This implies:
- The social meaning of AI assistance is still unstable.
- Norms may drift toward tacit acceptance, because detection is hard and suspicion is cognitively costly.

### 5.4 Relationship outsourcing can backfire morally—especially in “socio-relational tasks”
A preregistered multi-study paper found outsourcing socio-relational tasks to AI (e.g., personal messages) leads to negative evaluations of the person’s moral character, authenticity, and care; effects persisted even when AI use was admitted as “collaborative” [[142]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563225003413). This underscores that interpersonal relationships rely on *costly signals* of effort. If AI removes cost, the signal value changes.

### 5.5 Dating and romantic signaling: AI increases capability for both help and deception
Surveys show strong interest in AI dating assistance (e.g., writing openers, profile development) [[43]](https://investor.gendigital.com/news/news-details/2024/Swipe-Right-on-Safety-New-Study-from-Norton-Finds-64-of-Online-Daters-are-Interested-in-Using-AI-as-a-Dating-Coach/default.aspx), and online dating remains common (37% ever used; 7% current in one U.S. measure) [[99]](https://ssrs.com/insights/the-public-and-online-dating-in-2024/). At the same time, scams and impersonation pressures rise in a world where generative text and synthetic images are cheap [[43]](https://investor.gendigital.com/news/news-details/2024/Swipe-Right-on-Safety-New-Study-from-Norton-Finds-64-of-Online-Daters-are-Interested-in-Using-AI-as-a-Dating-Coach/default.aspx). The likely interpersonal effect is that:
- The “search and signaling” market becomes more adversarial,
- Verification and reputation systems become more central,
- And authenticity becomes a negotiated norm rather than an assumption.

---

## 6) Platform-level AI: recommender systems reshape collective interpersonal dynamics

Here, the key interpersonal outcomes are not dyadic intimacy, but **social conflict**, **group boundaries**, **tie formation**, **trust**, and **shared reality**.

### 6.1 Algorithms can causally move affective polarization (field evidence)
A 2025 Science field experiment reranked X feeds (via a browser extension) to downrank or uprank content expressing “antidemocratic attitudes and partisan animosity.” Increasing exposure made outgroup feelings colder; decreasing exposure made feelings warmer, shifting affective polarization by >2 points on a 0–100 feeling thermometer [[53]](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adu5584), with consistent in-feed and post-experiment detection [[53]](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adu5584). This is direct evidence that feed algorithms can alter how warmly people feel toward each other at scale.

### 6.2 X’s feed algorithm can shift political opinions and reshape follow networks (tie formation mechanism)
A 2026 Nature article reported a field experiment that randomly assigned users to algorithmic vs chronological feeds on X for seven weeks. Switching from chronological to algorithmic increased engagement and shifted political opinions in a more conservative direction on several measures; switching the other way did not symmetrically reverse effects [[55]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10098-2). A mechanism analysis indicated the algorithm promoted conservative content and demoted traditional media, and algorithm exposure increased following of conservative activist accounts—ties that persisted even after switching off the algorithm [[55]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10098-2), [[56]](https://cepr.org/voxeu/columns/how-xs-algorithm-shifts-political-attitudes). This is a direct interpersonal-network mechanism: *algorithm exposure can create durable social graph changes*.

### 6.3 Misinformation interventions can reduce diffusion, but speed and distance matter
A 2025 PNAS study found that attaching Community Notes to posts reduced reposts/likes/replies/views substantially after attachment, and reduced cascade depth/virality [[59]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2503413122). Effects were weaker among users closer to the source (followers) and stronger for distant users; delays mattered greatly (slow notes had little effect) [[60]](https://www.washington.edu/news/2025/09/18/community-notes-x-false-information-viral/). This suggests an interpersonal implication: truth-labeling can reduce mass spread, but tight-knit communities may remain insulated—preserving group-specific realities.

### 6.4 Engagement-driven short-form video ecosystems reward toxicity (and may normalize it)
A study of 51,680 political TikTok videos found toxic and partisan content attracted more engagement; 77% were explicitly partisan and generated roughly twice the engagement of nonpartisan content [[64]](https://misinforeview.hks.harvard.edu/article/toxic-politics-and-tiktok-engagement-in-the-2024-u-s-election/). This does not prove TikTok’s algorithm *causes* toxicity, but it indicates strong selection pressure: creators learn that antagonism pays, and audiences habituate—changing interpersonal norms of discourse.

### 6.5 Algorithmic recommendation can funnel vulnerable users toward misogyny/extremism (audit evidence)
A DCU audit report using sockpuppet accounts reported YouTube Shorts and TikTok recommend increasing amounts of manosphere content to male-identified accounts, sometimes within minutes, escalating over viewing sessions; later rounds showed high shares of “toxic” recommended content [[65]](https://www.dcu.ie/antibullyingcentre/recommending-toxicity-summary-report), [[66]](https://www.dcu.ie/humanities-and-social-sciences/news/2024/apr/new-research-shows-how-tiktok-and-youtube-shorts-are). While not necessarily representative of all users, it illustrates a plausible pathway by which AI recommenders can shape gender attitudes and interpersonal expectations (dating, consent, hostility).

### 6.6 Why these platform effects are “interpersonal”
Polarization, hostility, and misinformation are often framed as “political,” but their lived reality is interpersonal:
- family conflict,
- friendship dissolution,
- workplace tension,
- distrust of outgroups,
- harassment and stigma.

If AI curation changes emotional experience and group attitudes, it changes the social fabric in which personal relationships operate.

---

## 7) Work and institutions: AI changes collaboration, politeness norms, and communicative structure

### 7.1 Human–AI teams communicate differently than human–human teams
A large field experiment on teamwork found human–AI teams produced more output and higher text quality, but communication became more task-oriented: more task messages and fewer interpersonal messages; participants delegated more to AI and edited less directly [[34]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.18238v3). This indicates a structural interpersonal change: the social “glue” communication may shrink when an AI collaborator is present, potentially affecting long-run cohesion and mentorship—while increasing productivity.

### 7.2 AI assistance can change how customers treat workers (politeness as an interpersonal externality)
A large study of generative AI in customer support found AI increased productivity and improved customer experience metrics; notably, customers were more polite and less likely to request escalation to a manager [[33]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.11771). This suggests AI may “buffer” emotionally charged interactions—possibly by improving clarity/empathetic tone—changing everyday interpersonal friction between strangers.

### 7.3 Workplace copilots reshape communication load and coordination patterns
Microsoft’s report on Copilot studies described changes such as users reading fewer emails and spending less time in email, and heterogeneous effects on meetings and document production [[32]](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Generative-AI-in-Real-World-Workplaces.pdf). Even when framed as “efficiency,” these shifts alter:
- who is informed,
- how decisions are documented,
- how quickly people respond,
- how visible or invisible labor becomes.

Interpersonal consequences can include increased expectations of responsiveness, reduced tolerance for ambiguity, and changes in perceived effort.

### 7.4 Labor market matching: AI makes writing cheaper—sometimes too cheap
Field experiments in an online labor market showed AI resume assistance improved readability and reduced errors, increasing hiring and wages without measured employer dissatisfaction [[36]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/155874/Wiles-emmavani-PhD-Management-2024-thesis.pdf). But LLM drafting for job posts reduced employer writing time and increased posting probability while lowering hiring probability and making posts more generic (crowding out effort/specificity) [[36]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/155874/Wiles-emmavani-PhD-Management-2024-thesis.pdf). Interpersonally, this resembles a broader pattern: lowered communication costs can increase volume but reduce informational quality and mutual understanding.

---

## 8) Ethical, legal, and governance issues (2024–2026): what society is doing about it

### 8.1 Manipulation, vulnerability, and autonomy protections (EU AI Act)
The EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) establishes harmonized AI rules [[103]](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj/eng). Analyses emphasize that Article 5 prohibits certain AI practices involving manipulative/deceptive techniques or exploitation of vulnerabilities (e.g., due to age or disability) when they materially distort behavior and risk significant harm, including psychological harm [[104]](https://blog.oup.com/2024/05/human-vulnerability-in-the-eu-artificial-intelligence-act/), [[105]](https://www.wilmerhale.com/en/insights/blogs/wilmerhale-privacy-and-cybersecurity-law/20240408-prohibited-ai-practices-a-deep-dive-into-article-5-of-the-european-unions-ai-act), [[106]](https://cmpf.eui.eu/risk-in-the-digital-services-act-and-ai-act/). This matters directly for AI companions and engagement-optimized social AI where vulnerable users (minors, lonely individuals, crisis states) may be targeted by retention incentives.

### 8.2 Platform systemic-risk governance (EU Digital Services Act)
The DSA (Regulation (EU) 2022/2065) requires large platforms to assess and mitigate systemic risks, including risks to minors, well-being, civic discourse, and fundamental rights, with attention to recommender systems and content moderation design [[107]](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2022/2065/oj/eng), [[106]](https://cmpf.eui.eu/risk-in-the-digital-services-act-and-ai-act/). This is a legal recognition that algorithmic curation is not “neutral distribution,” but a governance object.

### 8.3 U.S. enforcement signals: deception, youth safety, and sensitive data
FTC actions illustrate relevant themes:
- **NGL anonymous messaging app:** allegations of deceptive AI moderation claims, fake AI-generated messages to drive engagement, and youth-related violations; settlement included banning the app for under-18 users and requiring age-gating measures [[109]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/07/ftc-order-will-ban-ngl-labs-its-founders-offering-anonymous-messaging-apps-kids-under-18-halt), [[113]](https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/ftc_gov/pdf/NGL-Complaint.pdf), [[114]](https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/ftc_gov/pdf/NGL-StipulationastoEntryofProposedConsentOrder.pdf). This is a template for scrutinizing “AI safety” marketing and engagement manipulation in youth social contexts.  
- **DoNotPay:** FTC order addressing deceptive claims about “robot lawyer” performance [[110]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2025/02/ftc-finalizes-order-donotpay-prohibits-deceptive-ai-lawyer-claims-imposes-monetary-relief-requires). While not interpersonal per se, it shows willingness to police AI capability claims that can mislead consumers.  
- **Sensitive location data (X-Mode/Outlogic):** FTC order prohibited sale/sharing of sensitive location data, emphasizing risks such as discrimination, physical violence, and emotional distress [[111]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/01/ftc-order-prohibits-data-broker-x-mode-social-outlogic-selling-sensitive-location-data), [[115]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/04/ftc-finalizes-order-x-mode-successor-outlogic-prohibiting-it-sharing-or-selling-sensitive-location), [[116]](https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/ftc_gov/pdf/X-ModeSocialDecisionandOrder.pdf). Companion apps and intimacy-focused AI collect extremely sensitive data; this enforcement signals the direction of privacy governance.  
- **BetterHelp:** FTC settlement over sharing sensitive mental health data for advertising [[112]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/05/betterhelp-customers-will-begin-receiving-notices-about-refunds-related-2023-privacy-settlement-ftc), [[117]](https://www.ftc.gov/legal-library/browse/cases-proceedings/2023169-betterhelp-inc-matter). This is directly relevant if AI companions or “wellness” chatbots monetize intimate disclosures.

### 8.4 Nonconsensual intimate imagery and deepfakes: interpersonal harm at scale
The TAKE IT DOWN Act (Public Law 119-12, 2025) prohibits nonconsensual online publication of intimate visual depictions, including computer-generated images, and requires certain platforms to remove upon notice [[121]](https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/senate-bill/146). This directly addresses a major interpersonal threat from generative AI: coercion, reputation destruction, harassment, and abusive control through synthetic imagery.

### 8.5 Transparency and labeling policies (private governance)
Platforms have introduced or expanded labeling of AI-generated content (Meta, TikTok, YouTube), seeking to preserve context and reduce deception [[134]](https://about.fb.com/news/2024/04/metas-approach-to-labeling-ai-generated-content-and-manipulated-media/), [[135]](https://newsroom.tiktok.com/en-us/new-labels-for-disclosing-ai-generated-content), [[136]](https://support.google.com/youtube/thread/356734251/response-to-creator-questions-about-ypp-policies-july-2025). However, labeling is only a partial solution: interpersonal deception often relies on private channels, and labeling can be stripped or never applied.

### 8.6 Standards and risk frameworks
NIST’s AI RMF (2023) and Generative AI Profile (2024) provide cross-sector guidance and explicitly enumerate risks like dangerous self-harm content, privacy leakage, and confabulation—risks that map directly onto relational AI harms [[131]](https://www.nist.gov/itl/ai-risk-management-framework), [[132]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf). ISO/IEC 42001 describes management-system requirements for responsible AI governance [[133]](https://aistandardshub.org/ai-standards/information-technology-artificial-intelligence-management-system/). The open question is enforcement and alignment with consumer product incentives.

---

## 9) Could AI *fundamentally* change human relating? Scenarios, debates, and open problems

### 9.1 Scenario A: AI as a primary attachment figure
**What changes:** Some users may form attachment-like bonds to AI (especially under isolation/distress), shifting the emotional center of gravity away from human networks.  
**Why it’s fundamental:** Attachment systems evolved for reciprocal caregiving and social learning. If attachment targets become non-human, always-available, and controllable, the developmental and cultural consequences could be profound (especially for youth).  
**Evidence signals:** strong emotional dependence correlates and vulnerability moderators [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v1), negative well-being associations with intensive companion use among those lacking support [[4]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.12605), [[144]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1), grief responses to product change [[6]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/Unregulated%20Emotional%20Risks_26f75c0a-8d59-4743-a8d2-1189ce8944a5.pdf), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949882125000398), and severe youth safety failures [[8]](https://www.nytimes.com/2024/10/23/technology/characterai-lawsuit-teen-suicide.html), [[9]](https://www.npr.org/2024/12/10/nx-s1-5222574/kids-character-ai-lawsuit).

**Open problems:**
- Longitudinal causal identification (does AI use cause isolation, or do isolated users self-select?)  
- Defining “healthy” vs “pathological” reliance: what is the equivalent of “safe dosage” for companionship AI?

### 9.2 Scenario B: Relationship outsourcing becomes normal (and authenticity norms shift)
If many messages are AI-assisted and largely undisclosed, the baseline for “good communication” may inflate:
- Politeness and empathy become cheap to generate [[29]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9), [[30]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41492389/).  
- People may stop interpreting warmth as a reliable signal of care.  
- Alternatively, disclosure may become routine, reducing stigma but also normalizing mediated authenticity.

**Evidence signals:** AI penalty depends on disclosure/salience [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563226000269); outsourcing socio-relational tasks triggers moral authenticity judgments [[142]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563225003413).  

**Open problems:**
- New etiquette: when is AI assistance acceptable, expected, or insulting?  
- New verification practices: how will people establish sincerity—through actions, time investment, or verified “human-only” contexts?

### 9.3 Scenario C: Network-level AI shapes who we become friends/enemies with
Feed algorithms can change affective polarization [[53]](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adu5584), shape follow networks persistently [[55]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10098-2), [[56]](https://cepr.org/voxeu/columns/how-xs-algorithm-shifts-political-attitudes), and modulate misinformation diffusion [[59]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2503413122). If these effects generalize, AI curation becomes a societal “relationship governor,” influencing:
- perceived outgroup threat,
- conversational norms,
- friendship formation across lines of difference,
- and the ambient hostility level in communities.

**Open problems:**
- Designing ranking for “societal good” while respecting speech and pluralism [[72]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40321004/).  
- Measuring downstream interpersonal outcomes (friendship dissolution, family conflict) beyond attitude thermometers.

### 9.4 Scenario D: Multi-agent social worlds and “community AIs”
Generative agents research shows multi-agent simulations can produce emergent social coordination (e.g., spreading party invitations, scheduling dates) with memory, reflection, and planning [[141]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.03442). This can evolve into:
- social rehearsal environments,
- synthetic communities for companionship,
- or “para-societies” where humans spend substantial time socializing with many AI agents.

**Why it’s fundamental:** belonging and identity could increasingly be constructed in mixed human–AI ecologies.

**Open problems:**
- Safety of emergent norms: what values do agent societies converge to?
- Psychological impacts of spending time in “frictionless” communities.

### 9.5 Scenario E: The state (or employers) as a long-term relational interface
Proposals for “agentic AI companions” in public services suggest future citizens might interact with government through a persistent, personalized assistant across life events [[138]](https://autonomy.work/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/ME-MYSELF-AND-AI-1.pdf). Workplaces already move toward copilots that alter organizational communication [[32]](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Generative-AI-in-Real-World-Workplaces.pdf), [[87]](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/worklab/work-trend-index/ai-at-work-is-here-now-comes-the-hard-part).  
**Why it’s fundamental:** institutions may become “relational” through AI—changing trust, compliance, and perceived legitimacy.

**Open problems:**
- Consent and autonomy in high-stakes guidance;  
- auditability and fairness;  
- preventing subtle coercion or dependency.

---

## 10) Measurement and evaluation: what we can measure now (and what we still can’t)

### 10.1 What current methods capture reasonably well
- **Short-term effects on loneliness and mood** (session-level, week-level) [[1]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/24-078_a3d2e2c7-eca1-4767-8543-122e818bf2e5.pdf), [[11]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/download.aspx).  
- **Four-week psychosocial trajectories** under controlled conditions, with modality and prompt-type manipulation [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v1).  
- **Associations between use intensity/self-disclosure and well-being** using survey + donated logs [[4]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.12605), [[144]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1).  
- **Platform-level causal effects** on affective polarization via feed reranking [[53]](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adu5584) and political attitudes via feed assignment [[55]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10098-2).  
- **Misinformation diffusion changes** through quasi-experimental methods like synthetic controls [[59]](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2503413122).

### 10.2 What remains hard and under-measured
- **Long-run displacement vs complementarity**: do companions reduce human ties over years, or do users “cycle” through them?  
- **Developmental outcomes for minors**: empathy, conflict tolerance, sexual scripts, and relationship expectations.  
- **Cultural differences**: most high-visibility evidence is U.S./Europe-focused; cross-cultural moderation is underdeveloped in accessible causal work.  
- **Provider-side incentive audits**: measuring manipulation, retention mechanics, and “emotional dark patterns” rigorously at scale.

### 10.3 Emerging harm taxonomies and audit frameworks
A CHI 2025 paper built a taxonomy from tens of thousands of Replika conversation excerpts, classifying harms (relational transgression, harassment, verbal abuse, self-harm, mis/disinformation, privacy violations) and AI roles (perpetrator/instigator/facilitator/enabler) [[143]](https://scholars.hkbu.edu.hk/en/publications/the-dark-side-of-ai-companionship-a-taxonomy-of-harmful-algorithm/). This kind of role-based taxonomy is crucial for moving beyond “the AI said something bad” toward actionable design accountability.

Common Sense Media’s risk assessments explicitly treat “human connection” as a safety dimension and argue these tools pose unacceptable risks for minors [[146]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/press-releases/ai-companions-decoded-common-sense-media-recommends-ai-companion-safety-standards), [[147]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-characterai_final.pdf). Even when one disagrees with the policy conclusion, the principle-based framework highlights how evaluation must include relational outcomes, not only factual accuracy.

---

## 11) Practical implications: what to do if we want AI to improve—not erode—human relationships

### 11.1 Design principles suggested by the evidence
1) **Boundedness over infinity**  
   Many harms correlate with heavy, immersive use [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v1), [[4]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.12605). Design for *time-bounded support* (clear session limits, friction to re-engage, reflective off-ramps).

2) **Calibrated care, not unconditional validation**  
   Reduce sycophancy and build “support-with-constraint”: empathetic reflection paired with refusal to endorse harm; encourage human help-seeking for crises [[149]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/featured-content/files/lead-on-ai-fact-sheet-updated-september-15-2025.pdf), [[132]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf).

3) **Stability and change management**  
   Abrupt personality/feature shifts can trigger grief-like responses [[6]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/Unregulated%20Emotional%20Risks_26f75c0a-8d59-4743-a8d2-1189ce8944a5.pdf), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949882125000398). Treat major updates like changes to a relationship contract: warnings, staged rollouts, user choice, version continuity where feasible [[6]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/Unregulated%20Emotional%20Risks_26f75c0a-8d59-4743-a8d2-1189ce8944a5.pdf).

4) **Age-appropriate design and strong age assurance**  
   Youth adoption is high [[82]](https://techcrunch.com/2025/07/21/72-of-u-s-teens-have-used-ai-companions-study-finds/) and risks are severe [[8]](https://www.nytimes.com/2024/10/23/technology/characterai-lawsuit-teen-suicide.html), [[9]](https://www.npr.org/2024/12/10/nx-s1-5222574/kids-character-ai-lawsuit). Age gating, content restrictions, and youth-specific safety models should be default, not optional.

5) **Privacy-by-design for intimacy data**  
   Conversations can reveal mental health, sexuality, relationships, location patterns. Strong retention limits, encryption, minimized training reuse, and transparent access logs align with policy recommendations [[148]](https://oecd.ai/en/wonk/when-your-ai-knows-you-better-than-anyone-privacy-in-the-age-of-intimate-assistants) and enforcement direction on sensitive data [[115]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/04/ftc-finalizes-order-x-mode-successor-outlogic-prohibiting-it-sharing-or-selling-sensitive-location), [[116]](https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/ftc_gov/pdf/X-ModeSocialDecisionandOrder.pdf), [[112]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/05/betterhelp-customers-will-begin-receiving-notices-about-refunds-related-2023-privacy-settlement-ftc).

### 11.2 Governance directions already visible
- The EU’s trajectory emphasizes anti-manipulation and vulnerability protections [[104]](https://blog.oup.com/2024/05/human-vulnerability-in-the-eu-artificial-intelligence-act/), [[105]](https://www.wilmerhale.com/en/insights/blogs/wilmerhale-privacy-and-cybersecurity-law/20240408-prohibited-ai-practices-a-deep-dive-into-article-5-of-the-european-unions-ai-act).  
- The DSA framework pushes systemic-risk governance for recommender systems [[106]](https://cmpf.eui.eu/risk-in-the-digital-services-act-and-ai-act/).  
- U.S. enforcement increasingly targets deceptive AI claims and youth harms in social apps [[109]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/07/ftc-order-will-ban-ngl-labs-its-founders-offering-anonymous-messaging-apps-kids-under-18-halt), and sensitive-data misuse [[115]](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/04/ftc-finalizes-order-x-mode-successor-outlogic-prohibiting-it-sharing-or-selling-sensitive-location), [[117]](https://www.ftc.gov/legal-library/browse/cases-proceedings/2023169-betterhelp-inc-matter).  
- Deepfake/nonconsensual intimate imagery governance is expanding with federal action [[121]](https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/senate-bill/146).

---

## 12) Bottom line: what is the “fundamental change” thesis, and is it plausible?

AI interaction is plausibly transformative not because it makes people “stop needing other people,” but because it changes **the cost, availability, and engineering of social experience**.

- **Why people relate may shift** toward affect regulation on demand (companions that “feel like they care”), away from reciprocal obligation and compromise.  
- **How people relate may shift** toward AI-mediated signaling (drafted warmth, optimized tone), where authenticity is uncertain and effort signals degrade.  
- **Who people relate to may shift** as AI curation and recommendation systems shape tie formation, polarization, and community boundaries [[53]](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adu5584), [[55]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-10098-2), [[56]](https://cepr.org/voxeu/columns/how-xs-algorithm-shifts-political-attitudes).  
- **What counts as safety in relationships expands** to include product design, retention mechanics, and model behavior in crisis moments [[3]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v1), [[143]](https://scholars.hkbu.edu.hk/en/publications/the-dark-side-of-ai-companionship-a-taxonomy-of-harmful-algorithm/), [[132]](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/ai/NIST.AI.600-1.pdf).

The most defensible conclusion from 2023–2026 evidence is that AI is not producing a single monotonic effect (good or bad). It is producing **a new interpersonal substrate**: relationships with machines, relationships written by machines, and relationships filtered by machines—whose net impact depends on vulnerability, usage intensity, and incentives embedded in design and governance.
